planMarkdown:
Bot: Private Early Crypto Coin Discovery Bot for Telegram

Summary:
A private grammY Telegram bot that monitors newly launched tokens on Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain, evaluates only three eligibility rules, and sends alerts exclusively to Telegram user 580632670.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js ESM service. It combines a Telegram command interface with a background blockchain scanner. It does not trade, custody funds, or make investment decisions. Its job is discovery and verification: find early token launches, estimate whether market capitalization is below 2000 USD, confirm ownership is renounced, confirm liquidity is locked, then alert the authorized user.

Product goals:
The product goal is to create a simple private alerting bot for very early crypto token discovery.

The bot should do the following:

1) Monitor Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain for newly created trading pairs involving newly launched tokens.

2) Estimate market capitalization and only consider tokens below 2000 USD market cap.

3) Verify that contract ownership is renounced. If renouncement cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

4) Verify that liquidity is locked. If the lock cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

5) Send a concise Telegram alert only to Telegram ID 580632670.

6) Provide simple private commands to check status, pause or resume scanning, run a manual scan, view recent alerts, and inspect a token.

The bot should prioritize correctness over volume. False positives are more dangerous than missed alerts, so the system should fail closed: if market cap, renouncement, or liquidity lock status cannot be confidently verified, no alert should be sent.

High-level architecture:
The service runs as one Node.js ESM process with two main responsibilities inside the same runtime.

1) Telegram interface: grammY handles commands, access control, status responses, manual scans, and alert delivery. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is read from environment variables. Every update is checked against the authorized Telegram ID 580632670 before any command is executed.

2) Background scanner: a scheduled worker polls configured EVM-compatible RPC endpoints for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain. It tracks block cursors per chain and scans new DEX pair creation events in small block ranges. The scanner evaluates candidate tokens, stores results, deduplicates alerts, and asks the Telegram layer to notify the owner when a token passes all criteria.

The blockchain monitoring approach should be event-driven by polling logs, not by unreliable chat messages or social sources. For each supported chain, a chain adapter defines the RPC endpoint, chain ID, wrapped native token address, common stable quote assets, DEX factory contracts to monitor, known liquidity locker contracts, burn addresses, and scanning parameters.

The token evaluation pipeline should be deterministic:

1) Discover new pair creation event.

2) Identify which side of the pair is likely the new token and which side is the quote asset.

3) Read token metadata such as name, symbol, decimals, total supply, and contract bytecode availability.

4) Estimate token price from DEX reserves and derive market cap from total supply multiplied by token price.

5) Reject if market cap is unknown or greater than or equal to 2000 USD.

6) Check ownership renouncement using standard ownership patterns. Accept only if ownership is zero address, dead address, or otherwise clearly renounced. Reject contracts with active owner, active admin, or unverifiable control.

7) Check liquidity lock status by inspecting LP token ownership. Accept only if the required majority of LP tokens is held by recognized lock contracts with a future unlock time or by burn addresses. A practical default is 95 percent or more of LP tokens locked or burned, because tiny residual LP balances are common. This percentage should be configurable but the alert rule remains liquidity must be locked.

8) Persist the decision and alert only once per chain and token address.

The architecture should include strong rate-limit handling. RPC calls should be batched where practical, retried with backoff, and bounded by timeouts. The scanner should keep persistent cursors so a restart does not rescan the entire chain or miss blocks already in progress.

Because this is a single-process bot, production readiness comes from idempotent jobs, persistent state, conservative verification, robust error handling, and simple operational commands rather than distributed infrastructure.

Data model:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence because the bot needs durable scan cursors, alert deduplication, and inspection history. In-memory maps are acceptable only for local testing, because they lose state on restart.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) settings: stores bot configuration such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, scan interval, paused flag, liquidity lock percentage, and authorized Telegram ID. The authorized ID should default to 580632670 and should not be editable by normal bot commands.

2) chaincursors: one document per chain and DEX factory being scanned. Stores last successfully scanned block, last scan time, and error metadata. This prevents duplicate work and supports safe restarts.

3) tokencandidates: one document per discovered token per chain. Stores chain, token address, pair address, factory address, quote asset, discovered block, discovered transaction hash, metadata, reserves, market cap estimate, ownership check result, liquidity lock check result, final eligibility status, rejection reason, and timestamps.

4) alerts: one document per sent alert. Stores chain, token address, pair address, market cap at alert time, lock details, renouncement details, Telegram message ID, and sent timestamp. A unique index on chain plus token address prevents duplicate alerts.

5) auditevents: records administrative actions such as pause, resume, manual scan, settings changes, and command usage by the authorized user. This helps troubleshoot bot behavior.

Important token fields:

1) chain: ethereum or bnbsmartchain.

2) tokenaddress: normalized lowercase contract address.

3) pairaddress: DEX liquidity pair address used for pricing and LP lock verification.

4) symbol, name, decimals, totalsupply.

5) marketcapusd: estimated current market capitalization.

6) ownershipstatus: renounced, activeowner, unsupported, unknown, or error.

7) liquiditystatus: locked, unlocked, partial, unsupported, unknown, or error.

8) eligible: true only when market cap is below 2000, ownership is renounced, and liquidity is locked.

9) rejectionreason: explains why no alert was sent.

10) firstseenat, lastcheckedat, alertedat.

Market cap estimates should include the calculation inputs so the user can understand the alert. For example, the stored record should retain reserve amounts, quote asset price source, total supply, and final estimated USD value.

Key flows:
Startup flow:

1) Load environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and RPC endpoint configuration.

2) Initialize MongoDB connection if persistence is enabled.

3) Register grammY commands and access control.

4) Load settings and chain cursors.

5) Start the Telegram bot polling or webhook mode, depending on deployment preference.

6) Start the background scanner loop if scanning is not paused.

Access control flow:

1) Every incoming Telegram update is inspected before command handling.

2) If the user ID is not 580632670, the bot replies with a generic unauthorized message or ignores the update.

3) No command, status data, settings, or alerts are exposed to any other Telegram user or group.

Discovery and alert flow:

1) Scanner reads new blocks since the last cursor.

2) Scanner fetches pair creation logs from configured DEX factories.

3) Candidate token is extracted from each new pair.

4) Token metadata and pair reserves are fetched.

5) Market cap is estimated. If it is not below 2000 USD, the candidate is rejected.

6) Ownership renouncement is checked. If not clearly renounced, the candidate is rejected.

7) LP token distribution and locker status are checked. If liquidity is not clearly locked or burned, the candidate is rejected.

8) If all checks pass, an alert record is inserted with a unique chain plus token constraint.

9) If the insert succeeds, the bot sends a Telegram alert to 580632670.

10) If the insert fails because the token was already alerted, no duplicate message is sent.

Manual scan flow:

1) The authorized user sends a scan command.

2) The bot starts a bounded scan for the requested chain or all enabled chains.

3) The bot returns a summary showing scanned blocks, candidate pairs found, eligible tokens, rejected tokens, errors, and next cursor position.

Token inspection flow:

1) The authorized user provides a chain and token address.

2) The bot fetches or refreshes metadata, market cap, ownership status, and liquidity lock status.

3) The bot returns a compact verdict explaining whether the token would qualify and why.

Error handling flow:

1) Temporary RPC failures are retried with exponential backoff.

2) Persistent failures are stored in chain cursor metadata and shown in the status command.

3) The scanner never advances a cursor beyond a block range that failed to process.

4) Alerts are sent only after data has been verified and persisted.

Tech stack:
The bot should use the following stack and no additional infrastructure beyond what is listed here.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY.

3) Telegram token: TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.

4) Persistence: MongoDB for production state, cursors, settings, candidate records, and alerts. In-memory maps may be used only for local development or disposable testing.

5) Blockchain access: EVM-compatible RPC endpoints configured through environment variables for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain.

6) Smart contract interaction: a standard EVM client library for reading logs, calling ERC-20 contracts, reading pair reserves, and checking ownership or LP token balances.

7) Scheduling: an in-process scanner loop using timers inside the same Node.js service.

8) File storage: not required for the core product. If later needed for reports or exports, use object storage or simple filesystem storage generically.

9) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud or a private server environment. Keep environment variables outside source control and run the process with restart supervision appropriate to the host.

No PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, external message queues, or additional databases are required. The design intentionally stays within one Node.js process plus MongoDB.

Non-functional requirements:
Security requirements:

1) Only Telegram user ID 580632670 can use the bot.

2) Secrets such as TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN, RPC URLs, and MongoDB connection details must be stored in environment variables.

3) The bot should never request private keys, seed phrases, wallet signatures, or trading permissions.

4) The bot should not expose debug data to unauthorized users.

Reliability requirements:

1) Scanner cursors must be durable so restarts do not lose progress.

2) Alert delivery must be idempotent so the same token is not repeatedly sent.

3) Failed block ranges should be retried instead of skipped.

4) RPC calls should use timeouts, retries, and conservative rate limits.

5) The scanner should process recent blocks with a small confirmation delay to reduce reorg-related mistakes.

Accuracy requirements:

1) The bot should reject unverifiable tokens rather than guess.

2) Market cap below 2000 USD should be treated as an estimate based on available DEX liquidity and quote asset pricing.

3) Renouncement detection is strongest for standard ownership patterns. Complex or nonstandard admin mechanisms may be impossible to prove, so they should be rejected.

4) Liquidity locked status depends on maintaining a trusted list of known locker contracts per chain and recognizing burn addresses.

Performance requirements:

1) The scanner should use bounded block ranges to avoid long blocking cycles.

2) It should avoid rescanning already processed pairs.

3) It should cache common token metadata and quote asset prices briefly to reduce RPC load.

4) Commands should remain responsive even while the scanner is running.

Operational requirements:

1) Provide status visibility through Telegram commands.

2) Log important scanner events, errors, rejected candidates, and sent alerts.

3) Keep configuration simple and editable only by the authorized user where appropriate.

4) Include a dry-run or verbose inspection mode for testing detection logic without sending noisy alerts.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initializes the private bot session and confirms whether the requester is authorized. (usage: /start) [access]
• /help — Shows available private commands and a short explanation of the alert criteria. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Shows scanner health, enabled chains, pause state, last scanned blocks, recent errors, and total alerts sent. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /pause — Pauses background scanning without stopping the Telegram bot. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resumes background scanning from the last saved cursors. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /scannow — Runs a bounded manual scan immediately for all enabled chains or for one selected chain. (usage: /scannow or /scannow ethereum or /scannow bnb) [scanner]
• /recent — Lists recent eligible token alerts with chain, symbol, market cap estimate, and alert time. (usage: /recent or /recent 10) [alerts]
• /inspect — Checks a specific token address and returns whether it meets the three criteria: below 2000 USD market cap, renounced, and liquidity locked. (usage: /inspect ethereum 0xTokenAddress or /inspect bnb 0xTokenAddress) [scanner]
• /settings — Displays current scan settings such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, liquidity lock percentage, scan interval, and confirmation delay. (usage: /settings) [configuration]
• /setthreshold — Updates the market cap threshold. The default target is 2000 USD. (usage: /setthreshold 2000) [configuration]

DOCS.md.content:
Crypto Sentinel is a private Telegram bot for one owner. It scans Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain for newly launched tokens and only sends an alert when all required criteria are positively verified.

Public commands:

1) /start
Shows the private bot introduction and explains what Crypto Sentinel scans for.
Usage: /start

2) /help
Shows commands, scan criteria, supported chains, and the financial risk notice.
Usage: /help

3) /status
Shows whether the scanner is running, whether a cycle is active, last scan time, scan interval, total saved alerts, and last critical error.
Usage: /status

4) /scan
Triggers one manual scan immediately and reports how many candidates were checked and how many alerts were sent.
Usage: /scan

Access control:
Only the configured owner can use the bot. By default, the owner is Telegram user ID 580632670. You can override it with ADMIN_TELEGRAM_ID. Unauthorized users receive only a short denial message and no scanner details.

Environment variables:

1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI
Required. MongoDB connection string used to store alerted tokens and scanner state.

3) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_ID
Optional. Defaults safely to 580632670.

4) SCAN_INTERVAL_MS
Optional. Defaults safely to 60000 milliseconds when missing or invalid.

Scanner criteria:
The bot only alerts when all of these are true:

1) Market cap or FDV is available and below $2,000.

2) Ownership is positively verified as renounced.

3) Liquidity is positively verified as locked.

The bot skips tokens with missing market cap, missing pair data, honeypot warnings, unverifiable ownership, unverifiable liquidity, active ownership, or unlocked liquidity.

Data sources:
Crypto Sentinel uses DexScreener public token and pair data for discovery and market data. It uses GoPlus Security public token security data for ownership, honeypot, and liquidity lock verification.

Database collections:

1) alertedTokens
Stores chain, tokenAddress, pairAddress, symbol, name, marketCap, liquidity, source URLs, firstSeenAt, createdAt, and updatedAt. A unique index on chain plus tokenAddress prevents duplicate alerts.

2) scannerState
Stores scanner running state, last scan time, scan interval, last checked counts, and last error.

Operational notes:
The scanner runs inside the same Node.js process as the Telegram bot. There is no separate worker or queue. The scanner uses a non-overlapping polling loop and sleeps after each cycle.

Limitations:
The bot only alerts when external data sources positively verify all requested criteria. Renounced ownership and locked liquidity checks depend on third-party and on-chain data availability. Market cap below $2,000 and locked liquidity do not make a token safe. This bot is informational only and not financial advice.

Setup:
Install dependencies with npm install. Copy .env.sample to .env and fill TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI. Run npm run dev locally or npm start in production.

Deployment:
On Render or a similar host, set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI. The build command is npm run build and the start command is npm start. The bot uses long polling and clears webhooks on startup.

Troubleshooting:
If the bot exits immediately, confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI are set. If alerts are missing, check /status and logs. Missing alerts usually mean one of the required criteria could not be positively verified by the public data sources.

aiMessages.content: Create a very simple telegram bot that discover early launched crypto coin on Binance block chain and Ethereum Blockchain when they market cap is below $2000 mc. The coins must be renounced and liquidity locked. this is the only criteria.  The bot must only be used by me with my telegram id: 580632670

Built Crypto Sentinel as a private Telegram-only grammY bot with MongoDB persistence, owner-only commands, a safe in-process scanner loop, DexScreener discovery, GoPlus verification, deduplicated alerts, and production-safe logging.

Files touched (21): .env.sample, DOCS.md, package.json, project.json, src/bot.js, src/commands/help.js…
Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Private Early Crypto Coin Discovery Bot for Telegram

Summary:
A private grammY Telegram bot that monitors newly launched tokens on Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain, evaluates only three eligibility rules, and sends alerts exclusively to Telegram user 580632670.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js ESM service. It combines a Telegram command interface with a background blockchain scanner. It does not trade, custody funds, or make investment decisions. Its job is discovery and verification: find early token launches, estimate whether market capitalization is below 2000 USD, confirm ownership is renounced, confirm liquidity is locked, then alert the authorized user.

Product goals:
The product goal is to create a simple private alerting bot for very early crypto token discovery.

The bot should do the following:

1) Monitor Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain for newly created trading pairs involving newly launched tokens.

2) Estimate market capitalization and only consider tokens below 2000 USD market cap.

3) Verify that contract ownership is renounced. If renouncement cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

4) Verify that liquidity is locked. If the lock cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

5) Send a concise Telegram alert only to Telegram ID 580632670.

6) Provide simple private commands to check status, pause or resume scanning, run a manual scan, view recent alerts, and inspect a token.

The bot should prioritize correctness over volume. False positives are more dangerous than missed alerts, so the system should fail closed: if market cap, renouncement, or liquidity lock status cannot be confidently verified, no alert should be sent.

High-level architecture:
The service runs as one Node.js ESM process with two main responsibilities inside the same runtime.

1) Telegram interface: grammY handles commands, access control, status responses, manual scans, and alert delivery. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is read from environment variables. Every update is checked against the authorized Telegram ID 580632670 before any command is executed.

2) Background scanner: a scheduled worker polls configured EVM-compatible RPC endpoints for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain. It tracks block cursors per chain and scans new DEX pair creation events in small block ranges. The scanner evaluates candidate tokens, stores results, deduplicates alerts, and asks the Telegram layer to notify the owner when a token passes all criteria.

The blockchain monitoring approach should be event-driven by polling logs, not by unreliable chat messages or social sources. For each supported chain, a chain adapter defines the RPC endpoint, chain ID, wrapped native token address, common stable quote assets, DEX factory contracts to monitor, known liquidity locker contracts, burn addresses, and scanning parameters.

The token evaluation pipeline should be deterministic:

1) Discover new pair creation event.

2) Identify which side of the pair is likely the new token and which side is the quote asset.

3) Read token metadata such as name, symbol, decimals, total supply, and contract bytecode availability.

4) Estimate token price from DEX reserves and derive market cap from total supply multiplied by token price.

5) Reject if market cap is unknown or greater than or equal to 2000 USD.

6) Check ownership renouncement using standard ownership patterns. Accept only if ownership is zero address, dead address, or otherwise clearly renounced. Reject contracts with active owner, active admin, or unverifiable control.

7) Check liquidity lock status by inspecting LP token ownership. Accept only if the required majority of LP tokens is held by recognized lock contracts with a future unlock time or by burn addresses. A practical default is 95 percent or more of LP tokens locked or burned, because tiny residual LP balances are common. This percentage should be configurable but the alert rule remains liquidity must be locked.

8) Persist the decision and alert only once per chain and token address.

The architecture should include strong rate-limit handling. RPC calls should be batched where practical, retried with backoff, and bounded by timeouts. The scanner should keep persistent cursors so a restart does not rescan the entire chain or miss blocks already in progress.

Because this is a single-process bot, production readiness comes from idempotent jobs, persistent state, conservative verification, robust error handling, and simple operational commands rather than distributed infrastructure.

Data model:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence because the bot needs durable scan cursors, alert deduplication, and inspection history. In-memory maps are acceptable only for local testing, because they lose state on restart.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) settings: stores bot configuration such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, scan interval, paused flag, liquidity lock percentage, and authorized Telegram ID. The authorized ID should default to 580632670 and should not be editable by normal bot commands.

2) chaincursors: one document per chain and DEX factory being scanned. Stores last successfully scanned block, last scan time, and error metadata. This prevents duplicate work and supports safe restarts.

3) tokencandidates: one document per discovered token per chain. Stores chain, token address, pair address, factory address, quote asset, discovered block, discovered transaction hash, metadata, reserves, market cap estimate, ownership check result, liquidity lock check result, final eligibility status, rejection reason, and timestamps.

4) alerts: one document per sent alert. Stores chain, token address, pair address, market cap at alert time, lock details, renouncement details, Telegram message ID, and sent timestamp. A unique index on chain plus token address prevents duplicate alerts.

5) auditevents: records administrative actions such as pause, resume, manual scan, settings changes, and command usage by the authorized user. This helps troubleshoot bot behavior.

Important token fields:

1) chain: ethereum or bnbsmartchain.

2) tokenaddress: normalized lowercase contract address.

3) pairaddress: DEX liquidity pair address used for pricing and LP lock verification.

4) symbol, name, decimals, totalsupply.

5) marketcapusd: estimated current market capitalization.

6) ownershipstatus: renounced, activeowner, unsupported, unknown, or error.

7) liquiditystatus: locked, unlocked, partial, unsupported, unknown, or error.

8) eligible: true only when market cap is below 2000, ownership is renounced, and liquidity is locked.

9) rejectionreason: explains why no alert was sent.

10) firstseenat, lastcheckedat, alertedat.

Market cap estimates should include the calculation inputs so the user can understand the alert. For example, the stored record should retain reserve amounts, quote asset price source, total supply, and final estimated USD value.

Key flows:
Startup flow:

1) Load environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and RPC endpoint configuration.

2) Initialize MongoDB connection if persistence is enabled.

3) Register grammY commands and access control.

4) Load settings and chain cursors.

5) Start the Telegram bot polling or webhook mode, depending on deployment preference.

6) Start the background scanner loop if scanning is not paused.

Access control flow:

1) Every incoming Telegram update is inspected before command handling.

2) If the user ID is not 580632670, the bot replies with a generic unauthorized message or ignores the update.

3) No command, status data, settings, or alerts are exposed to any other Telegram user or group.

Discovery and alert flow:

1) Scanner reads new blocks since the last cursor.

2) Scanner fetches pair creation logs from configured DEX factories.

3) Candidate token is extracted from each new pair.

4) Token metadata and pair reserves are fetched.

5) Market cap is estimated. If it is not below 2000 USD, the candidate is rejected.

6) Ownership renouncement is checked. If not clearly renounced, the candidate is rejected.

7) LP token distribution and locker status are checked. If liquidity is not clearly locked or burned, the candidate is rejected.

8) If all checks pass, an alert record is inserted with a unique chain plus token constraint.

9) If the insert succeeds, the bot sends a Telegram alert to 580632670.

10) If the insert fails because the token was already alerted, no duplicate message is sent.

Manual scan flow:

1) The authorized user sends a scan command.

2) The bot starts a bounded scan for the requested chain or all enabled chains.

3) The bot returns a summary showing scanned blocks, candidate pairs found, eligible tokens, rejected tokens, errors, and next cursor position.

Token inspection flow:

1) The authorized user provides a chain and token address.

2) The bot fetches or refreshes metadata, market cap, ownership status, and liquidity lock status.

3) The bot returns a compact verdict explaining whether the token would qualify and why.

Error handling flow:

1) Temporary RPC failures are retried with exponential backoff.

2) Persistent failures are stored in chain cursor metadata and shown in the status command.

3) The scanner never advances a cursor beyond a block range that failed to process.

4) Alerts are sent only after data has been verified and persisted.

Tech stack:
The bot should use the following stack and no additional infrastructure beyond what is listed here.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY.

3) Telegram token: TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.

4) Persistence: MongoDB for production state, cursors, settings, candidate records, and alerts. In-memory maps may be used only for local development or disposable testing.

5) Blockchain access: EVM-compatible RPC endpoints configured through environment variables for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain.

6) Smart contract interaction: a standard EVM client library for reading logs, calling ERC-20 contracts, reading pair reserves, and checking ownership or LP token balances.

7) Scheduling: an in-process scanner loop using timers inside the same Node.js service.

8) File storage: not required for the core product. If later needed for reports or exports, use object storage or simple filesystem storage generically.

9) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud or a private server environment. Keep environment variables outside source control and run the process with restart supervision appropriate to the host.

No PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, external message queues, or additional databases are required. The design intentionally stays within one Node.js process plus MongoDB.

Non-functional requirements:
Security requirements:

1) Only Telegram user ID 580632670 can use the bot.

2) Secrets such as TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN, RPC URLs, and MongoDB connection details must be stored in environment variables.

3) The bot should never request private keys, seed phrases, wallet signatures, or trading permissions.

4) The bot should not expose debug data to unauthorized users.

Reliability requirements:

1) Scanner cursors must be durable so restarts do not lose progress.

2) Alert delivery must be idempotent so the same token is not repeatedly sent.

3) Failed block ranges should be retried instead of skipped.

4) RPC calls should use timeouts, retries, and conservative rate limits.

5) The scanner should process recent blocks with a small confirmation delay to reduce reorg-related mistakes.

Accuracy requirements:

1) The bot should reject unverifiable tokens rather than guess.

2) Market cap below 2000 USD should be treated as an estimate based on available DEX liquidity and quote asset pricing.

3) Renouncement detection is strongest for standard ownership patterns. Complex or nonstandard admin mechanisms may be impossible to prove, so they should be rejected.

4) Liquidity locked status depends on maintaining a trusted list of known locker contracts per chain and recognizing burn addresses.

Performance requirements:

1) The scanner should use bounded block ranges to avoid long blocking cycles.

2) It should avoid rescanning already processed pairs.

3) It should cache common token metadata and quote asset prices briefly to reduce RPC load.

4) Commands should remain responsive even while the scanner is running.

Operational requirements:

1) Provide status visibility through Telegram commands.

2) Log important scanner events, errors, rejected candidates, and sent alerts.

3) Keep configuration simple and editable only by the authorized user where appropriate.

4) Include a dry-run or verbose inspection mode for testing detection logic without sending noisy alerts.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initializes the private bot session and confirms whether the requester is authorized. (usage: /start) [access]
• /help — Shows available private commands and a short explanation of the alert criteria. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Shows scanner health, enabled chains, pause state, last scanned blocks, recent errors, and total alerts sent. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /pause — Pauses background scanning without stopping the Telegram bot. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resumes background scanning from the last saved cursors. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /scannow — Runs a bounded manual scan immediately for all enabled chains or for one selected chain. (usage: /scannow or /scannow ethereum or /scannow bnb) [scanner]
• /recent — Lists recent eligible token alerts with chain, symbol, market cap estimate, and alert time. (usage: /recent or /recent 10) [alerts]
• /inspect — Checks a specific token address and returns whether it meets the three criteria: below 2000 USD market cap, renounced, and liquidity locked. (usage: /inspect ethereum 0xTokenAddress or /inspect bnb 0xTokenAddress) [scanner]
• /settings — Displays current scan settings such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, liquidity lock percentage, scan interval, and confirmation delay. (usage: /settings) [configuration]
• /setthreshold — Updates the market cap threshold. The default target is 2000 USD. (usage: /setthreshold 2000) [configuration]

DOCS.md.content:
Crypto Sentinel is a private Telegram bot for one owner. It scans Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain for newly launched tokens and only sends an alert when all required criteria are positively verified.

Public commands:

1) /start
Shows the private bot introduction and explains what Crypto Sentinel scans for.
Usage: /start

2) /help
Shows commands, scan criteria, supported chains, and the financial risk notice.
Usage: /help

3) /status
Shows whether the scanner is running, whether a cycle is active, last scan time, scan interval, total saved alerts, and last critical error.
Usage: /status

4) /scan
Triggers one manual scan immediately and reports how many candidates were checked and how many alerts were sent.
Usage: /scan

Access control:
Only the configured owner can use the bot. By default, the owner is Telegram user ID 580632670. You can override it with ADMIN_TELEGRAM_ID. Unauthorized users receive only a short denial message and no scanner details.

Environment variables:

1) TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN
Required. Telegram bot token from BotFather.

2) MONGODB_URI
Required. MongoDB connection string used to store alerted tokens and scanner state.

3) ADMIN_TELEGRAM_ID
Optional. Defaults safely to 580632670.

4) SCAN_INTERVAL_MS
Optional. Defaults safely to 60000 milliseconds when missing or invalid.

Scanner criteria:
The bot only alerts when all of these are true:

1) Market cap or FDV is available and below $2,000.

2) Ownership is positively verified as renounced.

3) Liquidity is positively verified as locked.

The bot skips tokens with missing market cap, missing pair data, honeypot warnings, unverifiable ownership, unverifiable liquidity, active ownership, or unlocked liquidity.

Data sources:
Crypto Sentinel uses DexScreener public token and pair data for discovery and market data. It uses GoPlus Security public token security data for ownership, honeypot, and liquidity lock verification.

Database collections:

1) alertedTokens
Stores chain, tokenAddress, pairAddress, symbol, name, marketCap, liquidity, source URLs, firstSeenAt, createdAt, and updatedAt. A unique index on chain plus tokenAddress prevents duplicate alerts.

2) scannerState
Stores scanner running state, last scan time, scan interval, last checked counts, and last error.

Operational notes:
The scanner runs inside the same Node.js process as the Telegram bot. There is no separate worker or queue. The scanner uses a non-overlapping polling loop and sleeps after each cycle.

Limitations:
The bot only alerts when external data sources positively verify all requested criteria. Renounced ownership and locked liquidity checks depend on third-party and on-chain data availability. Market cap below $2,000 and locked liquidity do not make a token safe. This bot is informational only and not financial advice.

Setup:
Install dependencies with npm install. Copy .env.sample to .env and fill TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI. Run npm run dev locally or npm start in production.

Deployment:
On Render or a similar host, set TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI. The build command is npm run build and the start command is npm start. The bot uses long polling and clears webhooks on startup.

Troubleshooting:
If the bot exits immediately, confirm TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN and MONGODB_URI are set. If alerts are missing, check /status and logs. Missing alerts usually mean one of the required criteria could not be positively verified by the public data sources.

aiMessages.content: Create a very simple telegram bot that discover early launched crypto coin on Binance block chain and Ethereum Blockchain when they market cap is below $2000 mc. The coins must be renounced and liquidity locked. this is the only criteria.  The bot must only be used by me with my telegram id: 580632670

Built Crypto Sentinel as a private Telegram-only grammY bot with MongoDB persistence, owner-only commands, a safe in-process scanner loop, DexScreener discovery, GoPlus verification, deduplicated alerts, and production-safe logging.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: Private Early Crypto Coin Discovery Bot for Telegram

Summary:
A private grammY Telegram bot that monitors newly launched tokens on Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain, evaluates only three eligibility rules, and sends alerts exclusively to Telegram user 580632670.

The bot is designed as a single Node.js ESM service. It combines a Telegram command interface with a background blockchain scanner. It does not trade, custody funds, or make investment decisions. Its job is discovery and verification: find early token launches, estimate whether market capitalization is below 2000 USD, confirm ownership is renounced, confirm liquidity is locked, then alert the authorized user.

Product goals:
The product goal is to create a simple private alerting bot for very early crypto token discovery.

The bot should do the following:

1) Monitor Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain for newly created trading pairs involving newly launched tokens.

2) Estimate market capitalization and only consider tokens below 2000 USD market cap.

3) Verify that contract ownership is renounced. If renouncement cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

4) Verify that liquidity is locked. If the lock cannot be verified, the token is rejected instead of alerted.

5) Send a concise Telegram alert only to Telegram ID 580632670.

6) Provide simple private commands to check status, pause or resume scanning, run a manual scan, view recent alerts, and inspect a token.

The bot should prioritize correctness over volume. False positives are more dangerous than missed alerts, so the system should fail closed: if market cap, renouncement, or liquidity lock status cannot be confidently verified, no alert should be sent.

High-level architecture:
The service runs as one Node.js ESM process with two main responsibilities inside the same runtime.

1) Telegram interface: grammY handles commands, access control, status responses, manual scans, and alert delivery. TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN is read from environment variables. Every update is checked against the authorized Telegram ID 580632670 before any command is executed.

2) Background scanner: a scheduled worker polls configured EVM-compatible RPC endpoints for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain. It tracks block cursors per chain and scans new DEX pair creation events in small block ranges. The scanner evaluates candidate tokens, stores results, deduplicates alerts, and asks the Telegram layer to notify the owner when a token passes all criteria.

The blockchain monitoring approach should be event-driven by polling logs, not by unreliable chat messages or social sources. For each supported chain, a chain adapter defines the RPC endpoint, chain ID, wrapped native token address, common stable quote assets, DEX factory contracts to monitor, known liquidity locker contracts, burn addresses, and scanning parameters.

The token evaluation pipeline should be deterministic:

1) Discover new pair creation event.

2) Identify which side of the pair is likely the new token and which side is the quote asset.

3) Read token metadata such as name, symbol, decimals, total supply, and contract bytecode availability.

4) Estimate token price from DEX reserves and derive market cap from total supply multiplied by token price.

5) Reject if market cap is unknown or greater than or equal to 2000 USD.

6) Check ownership renouncement using standard ownership patterns. Accept only if ownership is zero address, dead address, or otherwise clearly renounced. Reject contracts with active owner, active admin, or unverifiable control.

7) Check liquidity lock status by inspecting LP token ownership. Accept only if the required majority of LP tokens is held by recognized lock contracts with a future unlock time or by burn addresses. A practical default is 95 percent or more of LP tokens locked or burned, because tiny residual LP balances are common. This percentage should be configurable but the alert rule remains liquidity must be locked.

8) Persist the decision and alert only once per chain and token address.

The architecture should include strong rate-limit handling. RPC calls should be batched where practical, retried with backoff, and bounded by timeouts. The scanner should keep persistent cursors so a restart does not rescan the entire chain or miss blocks already in progress.

Because this is a single-process bot, production readiness comes from idempotent jobs, persistent state, conservative verification, robust error handling, and simple operational commands rather than distributed infrastructure.

Data model:
MongoDB is recommended for production persistence because the bot needs durable scan cursors, alert deduplication, and inspection history. In-memory maps are acceptable only for local testing, because they lose state on restart.

Suggested MongoDB collections:

1) settings: stores bot configuration such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, scan interval, paused flag, liquidity lock percentage, and authorized Telegram ID. The authorized ID should default to 580632670 and should not be editable by normal bot commands.

2) chaincursors: one document per chain and DEX factory being scanned. Stores last successfully scanned block, last scan time, and error metadata. This prevents duplicate work and supports safe restarts.

3) tokencandidates: one document per discovered token per chain. Stores chain, token address, pair address, factory address, quote asset, discovered block, discovered transaction hash, metadata, reserves, market cap estimate, ownership check result, liquidity lock check result, final eligibility status, rejection reason, and timestamps.

4) alerts: one document per sent alert. Stores chain, token address, pair address, market cap at alert time, lock details, renouncement details, Telegram message ID, and sent timestamp. A unique index on chain plus token address prevents duplicate alerts.

5) auditevents: records administrative actions such as pause, resume, manual scan, settings changes, and command usage by the authorized user. This helps troubleshoot bot behavior.

Important token fields:

1) chain: ethereum or bnbsmartchain.

2) tokenaddress: normalized lowercase contract address.

3) pairaddress: DEX liquidity pair address used for pricing and LP lock verification.

4) symbol, name, decimals, totalsupply.

5) marketcapusd: estimated current market capitalization.

6) ownershipstatus: renounced, activeowner, unsupported, unknown, or error.

7) liquiditystatus: locked, unlocked, partial, unsupported, unknown, or error.

8) eligible: true only when market cap is below 2000, ownership is renounced, and liquidity is locked.

9) rejectionreason: explains why no alert was sent.

10) firstseenat, lastcheckedat, alertedat.

Market cap estimates should include the calculation inputs so the user can understand the alert. For example, the stored record should retain reserve amounts, quote asset price source, total supply, and final estimated USD value.

Key flows:
Startup flow:

1) Load environment variables, including TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN and RPC endpoint configuration.

2) Initialize MongoDB connection if persistence is enabled.

3) Register grammY commands and access control.

4) Load settings and chain cursors.

5) Start the Telegram bot polling or webhook mode, depending on deployment preference.

6) Start the background scanner loop if scanning is not paused.

Access control flow:

1) Every incoming Telegram update is inspected before command handling.

2) If the user ID is not 580632670, the bot replies with a generic unauthorized message or ignores the update.

3) No command, status data, settings, or alerts are exposed to any other Telegram user or group.

Discovery and alert flow:

1) Scanner reads new blocks since the last cursor.

2) Scanner fetches pair creation logs from configured DEX factories.

3) Candidate token is extracted from each new pair.

4) Token metadata and pair reserves are fetched.

5) Market cap is estimated. If it is not below 2000 USD, the candidate is rejected.

6) Ownership renouncement is checked. If not clearly renounced, the candidate is rejected.

7) LP token distribution and locker status are checked. If liquidity is not clearly locked or burned, the candidate is rejected.

8) If all checks pass, an alert record is inserted with a unique chain plus token constraint.

9) If the insert succeeds, the bot sends a Telegram alert to 580632670.

10) If the insert fails because the token was already alerted, no duplicate message is sent.

Manual scan flow:

1) The authorized user sends a scan command.

2) The bot starts a bounded scan for the requested chain or all enabled chains.

3) The bot returns a summary showing scanned blocks, candidate pairs found, eligible tokens, rejected tokens, errors, and next cursor position.

Token inspection flow:

1) The authorized user provides a chain and token address.

2) The bot fetches or refreshes metadata, market cap, ownership status, and liquidity lock status.

3) The bot returns a compact verdict explaining whether the token would qualify and why.

Error handling flow:

1) Temporary RPC failures are retried with exponential backoff.

2) Persistent failures are stored in chain cursor metadata and shown in the status command.

3) The scanner never advances a cursor beyond a block range that failed to process.

4) Alerts are sent only after data has been verified and persisted.

Tech stack:
The bot should use the following stack and no additional infrastructure beyond what is listed here.

1) Runtime: Node.js using ESM modules.

2) Telegram framework: grammY.

3) Telegram token: TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN from environment variables.

4) Persistence: MongoDB for production state, cursors, settings, candidate records, and alerts. In-memory maps may be used only for local development or disposable testing.

5) Blockchain access: EVM-compatible RPC endpoints configured through environment variables for Ethereum and BNB Smart Chain.

6) Smart contract interaction: a standard EVM client library for reading logs, calling ERC-20 contracts, reading pair reserves, and checking ownership or LP token balances.

7) Scheduling: an in-process scanner loop using timers inside the same Node.js service.

8) File storage: not required for the core product. If later needed for reports or exports, use object storage or simple filesystem storage generically.

9) Deployment: a single Node.js server in the cloud or a private server environment. Keep environment variables outside source control and run the process with restart supervision appropriate to the host.

No PostgreSQL, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, RabbitMQ, external message queues, or additional databases are required. The design intentionally stays within one Node.js process plus MongoDB.

Non-functional requirements:
Security requirements:

1) Only Telegram user ID 580632670 can use the bot.

2) Secrets such as TELEGRAMBOTTOKEN, RPC URLs, and MongoDB connection details must be stored in environment variables.

3) The bot should never request private keys, seed phrases, wallet signatures, or trading permissions.

4) The bot should not expose debug data to unauthorized users.

Reliability requirements:

1) Scanner cursors must be durable so restarts do not lose progress.

2) Alert delivery must be idempotent so the same token is not repeatedly sent.

3) Failed block ranges should be retried instead of skipped.

4) RPC calls should use timeouts, retries, and conservative rate limits.

5) The scanner should process recent blocks with a small confirmation delay to reduce reorg-related mistakes.

Accuracy requirements:

1) The bot should reject unverifiable tokens rather than guess.

2) Market cap below 2000 USD should be treated as an estimate based on available DEX liquidity and quote asset pricing.

3) Renouncement detection is strongest for standard ownership patterns. Complex or nonstandard admin mechanisms may be impossible to prove, so they should be rejected.

4) Liquidity locked status depends on maintaining a trusted list of known locker contracts per chain and recognizing burn addresses.

Performance requirements:

1) The scanner should use bounded block ranges to avoid long blocking cycles.

2) It should avoid rescanning already processed pairs.

3) It should cache common token metadata and quote asset prices briefly to reduce RPC load.

4) Commands should remain responsive even while the scanner is running.

Operational requirements:

1) Provide status visibility through Telegram commands.

2) Log important scanner events, errors, rejected candidates, and sent alerts.

3) Keep configuration simple and editable only by the authorized user where appropriate.

4) Include a dry-run or verbose inspection mode for testing detection logic without sending noisy alerts.

Bot commands:
• /start — Initializes the private bot session and confirms whether the requester is authorized. (usage: /start) [access]
• /help — Shows available private commands and a short explanation of the alert criteria. (usage: /help) [general]
• /status — Shows scanner health, enabled chains, pause state, last scanned blocks, recent errors, and total alerts sent. (usage: /status) [operations]
• /pause — Pauses background scanning without stopping the Telegram bot. (usage: /pause) [operations]
• /resume — Resumes background scanning from the last saved cursors. (usage: /resume) [operations]
• /scannow — Runs a bounded manual scan immediately for all enabled chains or for one selected chain. (usage: /scannow or /scannow ethereum or /scannow bnb) [scanner]
• /recent — Lists recent eligible token alerts with chain, symbol, market cap estimate, and alert time. (usage: /recent or /recent 10) [alerts]
• /inspect — Checks a specific token address and returns whether it meets the three criteria: below 2000 USD market cap, renounced, and liquidity locked. (usage: /inspect ethereum 0xTokenAddress or /inspect bnb 0xTokenAddress) [scanner]
• /settings — Displays current scan settings such as enabled chains, market cap threshold, liquidity lock percentage, scan interval, and confirmation delay. (usage: /settings) [configuration]
• /setthreshold — Updates the market cap threshold. The default target is 2000 USD. (usage: /setthreshold 2000) [configuration]
